answer:
**"Eardstapa" appears in *The Wanderer* (lines 5b–6a of the Old English poem):**  
*Swa cwæð eardstapa, earfeþa gemyndig*[^1]  
("So spoke the earth-stepper, mindful of hardships"[^2]).  

This occurs at the start of the wanderer's monologue, immediately following the opening reflection on *wyrd* ("fate") as unyielding amid exile.[^3]  

The compound *eardstapa* fuses *eard* ("earth, land, native place, home") and *stapa* ("stepper, tramper"), evoking a figure who physically treads the earth in hardship-bound motion.[^4] Modern English "wanderer" (the poem's title) flattens this into a vague roamer, stripping the kenning's layered purchase:  

- **Earth/home as lost ground**: *Eard* implies not just soil but homeland (*eard* as "dwelling"), underscoring exile (*wræclastas*) from rooted life—treading what was once "home" now alienates.[^5]  
- **Stepper's endurance**: *Stapa* connotes laborious foot-travel over terrain, tying to the poem's imagery of rime-cold seas, ice-bound waves, and fallow paths; it buys grim, embodied motion versus abstract drifting.[^6]  

Together, it densifies the exile's plight: a home-lost stepper, earth as both path and reminder of loss. "Wanderer" cannot convey this native poetic economy, where compounds encode cultural exile (lordless, kinless) in two syllables.[^7]  

[^1]: *Unknown*, *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [p-9bd9cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/original/#p-9bd9cc).  
[^2]: Ibid., [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).  
[^3]: Ibid., full chapter [translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).  
[^4]: *Eardstapa* etymology from context and standard Old English lexicography (e.g., *Bosworth-Toller*); see [p-9bd9cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/original/#p-9bd9cc).  
[^5]: Cf. *eard* in "eardgeard" ("earth-realm," line 85); [full original](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/original/).  
[^6]: Echoes opening "wadan wræclastas" ("tread exile-paths"); [p-9cca5e](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9cca5e).  
[^7]: Full monologue [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
